transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a small rural farming community, the kind of place where everyone knew each other and the seasons really guided your life. As a child, there was always work to do—helping around the home, being out in the fields in some way—and you learned to be practical and not waste anything. School mattered to me, but it wasn’t something everyone expected you to finish, especially beyond the basics, so I had to be steady about it and keep going. We didn’t have much, but we had a strong sense of community, and people looked out for one another when someone was sick or struggling. Looking back, that’s probably where I first learned to pay attention to health and to neighbors, even before I had words for it.
What was the name of the rural community or the nearest town where you grew up, and what do you remember about times when neighbors came together to help someone who was sick or struggling there?

It was outside a small town called San Miguel—more countryside than town, really, but that’s what we said when people asked where we were from.

I remember that when someone got sick, it wasn’t just “their problem.” Neighbors would show up with soup or tortillas, or they’d take turns helping with chores—feeding animals, watering, bringing in whatever needed bringing in—so the family could stay close to the person who was ill. If it was something serious, people would also help arrange a ride into the nearest clinic or send someone to fetch the nurse, because not everyone had a way to travel. And when a family was struggling, someone always seemed to quietly leave a bag of beans or a little money—no big talk, just help. That feeling of everyone watching out for each other stayed with me.

That picture of San Miguel and neighbors quietly stepping in really comes through. About how old were you when you most remember those kinds of community efforts—was it when you were quite young, or more in your teenage years?

Mostly when I was quite young, maybe around seven or eight and into my early teens. Those are the years I remember most clearly because I was old enough to notice what was happening, and sometimes I was asked to carry something over or help with a small chore when a neighbor was sick. By my teenage years it was still happening, but it felt more like the normal way of life than something you stop and think about. I’d say the strongest memories are from childhood, when it first really made an impression on me.

When you were in your early teens and helping felt like a normal way of life, where exactly were you living then—still out in the countryside near San Miguel, or had your family moved closer into town or somewhere else?

We were still out in the countryside near San Miguel then. We hadn’t moved into town yet, so it was the same kind of life—fields nearby, neighbors not too far but still spread out, and you’d walk over when someone needed help. San Miguel was where we went for church or errands, but home was out on the rural side.
When you’d go into San Miguel for church or errands while living out in the countryside, how far was it from your home, and how did you usually travel there?

It wasn’t terribly far, but it felt like a trip because the roads weren’t great—maybe several kilometers, something like that. Most of the time we went on foot if it was just one of us and we weren’t carrying much, especially for church. If we had more to bring back, or if someone older was going, we’d go on whatever ride we could manage—sometimes a neighbor’s cart or a truck if someone was heading that way. And if there was a real need, like a sickness, you’d find a way to get a lift because walking wasn’t always possible.
When you say “we” would walk to church or get a ride into San Miguel, who was usually included in that—your parents, siblings, grandparents, or others in the household?

Most of the time “we” meant my parents and us children—my brothers and sisters—going together, especially for church. If it was just an errand, sometimes it would be only one of my parents and one of us kids, depending on what needed to be done. My grandparents weren’t usually in our household, so they weren’t part of that regular walk, though older relatives might come along once in a while if they were staying with us. And when it was a ride in someone’s cart or truck, it could include a neighbor too, because people would share whatever space they had.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a coastal city, so the sea was always part of life—its smell, the wind, the way everything moved with the seasons. As a child, our days were pretty simple and structured: school, helping at home, and running errands, with neighbors looking out for each other. We didn’t have much in the way of luxuries, but there was a strong sense of responsibility, and you learned early to be useful. I was always curious about how things worked, especially anything electrical or mechanical, and I liked watching the older men fix radios, lights, and appliances. Looking back, it was a practical upbringing—steady, modest, and very grounded in community.

That sounds like a steady, community-centered childhood, and I can really picture the sea shaping daily life.  

Which coastal city did you grow up in?

I grew up in a coastal city, but to be honest I usually keep the name private when I’m speaking generally like this. What mattered most was that it was a close-knit place by the sea—small enough that everyone knew each other, and the port and the markets shaped the rhythm of daily life. The sea was always the background to everything we did.

Of course—I understand keeping the name private, thank you for saying that.  

When you think of the port and the markets shaping the rhythm of life, what’s one specific memory or scene from childhood that captures that—something you saw, heard, or did as part of a typical day?

I can still see an early morning when my mother sent me with a small list and a few coins, and I’d walk toward the market while the port was already waking up. You’d hear the calls of the fish sellers mixing with the sound of crates being dragged and boats knocking softly against the dock, and everything smelled like sea air and fresh catch. Men would be unloading and arguing prices before the sun was fully up, and the shopkeepers would sprinkle water in front of their doors to keep the dust down. I remember standing there a moment longer than I should, watching the lamps and wires over the stalls swaying in the wind and thinking, “How does all of this stay working?” Then I’d hurry back home because in those days you didn’t keep people waiting—errands were a responsibility, not a suggestion.

That’s a vivid scene—the sounds at the dock, the water sprinkled to settle the dust, and you pausing to study the lamps and wires.  

About how old were you when your mother sent you on those early-morning market errands?

I was around eight or nine, I’d say. Not every morning, of course, but often enough that it felt normal to be out early with a little list in my hand. At that age you were trusted to do small things properly and come straight back. I can’t swear to the exact year, but I’m fairly sure it was before I turned ten.

Thank you—that helps place it, and it’s clear those responsibilities came early.  

When you went to the market at eight or nine, what kinds of items were usually on your mother’s list?

Usually it was the basics for the day—things that couldn’t wait. Fish if it was a good morning at the docks, some vegetables, a bit of bread, maybe onions or tomatoes, and sometimes tea or sugar if we were running low. If there was a neighbor who had just come by, she might add something small like soap or kerosene for a lamp, the kinds of household things you notice only when they’re finished. It was never a long list, but it had to be done correctly, and you learned to check the coins twice and come straight back.

You’re describing a very practical kind of trust—small lists, but accuracy mattered, and even neighbors relied on you.  

When you say you learned to “check the coins twice,” what was the reason—were you worried about being short, getting the wrong change, or something else?

It was mostly about not coming up short and not putting the seller in a position to argue with a child. We didn’t have extra money to cover a mistake, so if you miscounted, you’d feel it at home right away. And in a busy market, people move fast—if you didn’t pay attention, you could get the wrong change or lose a coin without even noticing. Checking twice was just a way of being careful and showing you could be trusted with what you were given.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a home where more than one language was spoken, so there was always a mix of voices and expressions around me. As a child, life felt busy but close-knit—family was very involved in day-to-day routines, and everyone looked out for one another. I remember being curious and fairly responsible early on, the kind of child who would notice if someone wasn’t feeling well or if something needed to be organized. A big part of my childhood was learning to adapt and listen, because with different languages and generations under one roof, you learned patience and respect. That environment shaped me a lot, especially the way I later gravitated toward caring work.

Thank you—that sounds like a lively, warm home, and it makes sense that it shaped your caring nature later on.  

Where exactly did you grow up (town/city and region), and what kind of home was it—an apartment, a house, or something else?

I grew up in Kerala, in the southern part of India, in a small town setting rather than a big city. We lived in a family house—nothing fancy, but it was a proper home with enough space that it always felt like someone was coming or going. Because we had multiple generations around, it didn’t feel “quiet” the way some homes do; it was very lived-in and active. I don’t remember thinking much about the type of home at the time—I just remember it as the place where everyone gathered and routines happened.

I can really picture that—always some movement, and the house as the center of family life.  

About how old were you during those years in that small town in Kerala, and roughly what years are we talking about?

Those “small town in Kerala” years were basically my whole childhood and teens—so from when I was born up through around 17 or 18, before I left for nursing studies. I was born in 1956, so we’re talking roughly the late 1950s through the early-to-mid 1970s. After that, I started spending more time away from home for training and work, and life became more city- and hospital-centered.

That’s very helpful—thank you for anchoring it with the years. When your life became more city- and hospital-centered for training and work, where did you go—what city or cities were you living in then?

For my nursing training and those early working years, I was mostly in bigger cities compared to my hometown—mainly in Kerala’s larger centers, and then later in other cities as my husband’s engineering postings moved us around. I don’t always remember every place in a neat timeline now, but the big change was going from a small town to a much more crowded, fast-paced hospital environment in the city. After I qualified and started working, I spent a lot of time around urban hospitals, and that became my “home base” feeling for a while, even when we relocated.

I understand—those moves can blur together over time, especially when work is so consuming. When you relocated because of your husband’s engineering postings, what were the main cities or places you lived, even if you list them without worrying about the exact order?

The ones that stand out most for me are Kochi and Thiruvananthapuram back in Kerala, and then a few stretches outside the state—Bengaluru and Chennai especially. There may have been a shorter posting or two in between, but those are the main places I remember actually setting up a household and adjusting my work around his projects. With each move, I’d usually look for a hospital or clinic where I could plug in quickly, so the city names stick more than the exact neighborhoods.
Thank you—that gives a clear set of places. About when did you live in Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram, Bengaluru, and Chennai—could you give approximate years or your age for each, even just roughly?

Roughly, I’d place Thiruvananthapuram first—around my late teens into my early 20s, so maybe about 1974 to 1980 or so, when I was in training and just starting out. Kochi was more in my 20s and into my early 30s—approximately the early 1980s up to the late 1980s.

After I married in 1986, the out-of-state stretches happened with my husband’s postings. Bengaluru I remember as being in my 30s—so somewhere in the early-to-mid 1990s. Chennai was later than that, more in my 40s—so late 1990s into the early 2000s, give or take.

I’m a little fuzzy on the exact year-to-year sequence, but that’s the general age range that feels right to me.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in Japan in a fairly modest neighborhood, the kind of place where you knew the shopkeepers and your neighbors watched out for you. As a child, life felt simple but disciplined—school, chores, and helping my family when I could. My father worked with wood, and I spent a lot of time watching him and learning to use my hands carefully, little by little. I wasn’t a loud or flashy kid; I was the one who could sit for a long time and focus, which later suited me well. Looking back, those early years taught me patience and respect for doing things properly, even when nobody is watching.

That sounds like a close-knit place, and it’s easy to picture you quietly watching your father and learning step by step.  

When you were spending that time with your father working with wood, where was that—was it in a home workshop, a small shop in the neighborhood, or somewhere else?

Mostly it was at home, in a small workshop space my father kept—more like a shed or a back room than a proper storefront. He had his tools arranged very neatly, and I learned early that you put everything back where it belongs. Sometimes he’d take on small jobs for neighbors, so people would come by with a broken drawer or a sliding door that didn’t fit right, but the work itself was usually done right there at home. I don’t remember it as a “shop” in the commercial sense—just our place, with the smell of wood and shavings on the floor.

I can really picture that—more a careful, well-kept home workspace than a storefront, with neighbors stopping by and that smell of fresh shavings.

About how old were you when your father first started letting you help in that workshop, rather than just watching?

I was probably around eight or nine when he first started letting me help in a real way. At first it was small things—sweeping up the shavings, holding a piece steady, handing him the right tool when he asked. He didn’t rush it, and he watched my hands more than my face, to make sure I was being careful. By the time I was maybe ten or so, he’d let me do simple sanding or measure and mark lines, but only after I’d shown I could be patient and not get careless.

You’re describing such a thoughtful way of teaching—earning trust through careful hands and steady attention.

When you were about ten and he started letting you sand and measure/mark lines, where exactly were you working—still in that same home shed/back room, or sometimes at a neighbor’s place or another location?

Mostly it was still in that same little workshop at home—the shed/back room space. Even when a neighbor had a problem, my father usually had them bring the drawer or the sliding door over to our place, so we could work with all his tools laid out the way he liked. Once in a while he would go over to someone’s house if it was something too awkward to move, like a door that was hanging badly, and I might come along and just hold things or watch. But the sanding and measuring he trusted me with, that was almost always done at home, where he could keep an eye on my hands and the workbench was steady.

Thank you—that makes sense, especially with him wanting everything set up just right at home.

Do you remember one particular item a neighbor brought over, or a specific repair you and your father worked on together that really stuck in your mind?

Yes, one that still comes back to me is a sliding door from a neighbor’s house—one of those light wooden doors with paper panels. They brought it over because it kept sticking and wouldn’t sit properly in the track, and the neighbor was getting frustrated with it.

My father had me help lay it flat and check the measurements, and then he showed me how even a small warp or a swollen edge can cause trouble. I remember doing the sanding very slowly along the edge while he kept checking the fit, telling me not to rush just because it looked like “only a little” needed to come off. When we put it back on the track and it finally moved smoothly, it felt like a big accomplishment, even though it was a small job. That kind of quiet satisfaction stayed with me.

That’s a vivid memory—the slow, careful sanding and then that moment when the door finally glides smoothly again.

When this sliding-door repair happened, do you remember roughly when it was—about what age you were, and what neighborhood or town you were living in at the time?

I’m fairly sure I was around ten, maybe just a little older—still a primary school boy, not yet in my teens. It was in the same modest neighborhood where we knew the shopkeepers and people looked out for each other, and neighbors would come by our place when something in the house wasn’t fitting right.

I wish I could tell you the exact town name without hesitation, but after so many years it’s a little blurry for me. What I remember clearly is the feel of that close, everyday community and that we were still living in the area where my father did those small repairs from our home workshop.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a small rural area where most people knew each other, and life moved at a steady pace. My family had a little shop, so from quite a young age I was helping out—packing shelves, serving customers, and learning how to be responsible even when I would rather be outside playing. We didn’t have a lot, but we made do, and there was a strong sense of community, like everyone looked out for one another. School was important in our house, even though there were always chores waiting, and I remember having to be disciplined about finishing my work. Looking back, that mix of hard work and people pulling together shaped me, and it’s part of why I later cared so much about children—especially girls—getting the chance to learn.

It sounds like you were balancing a lot from an early age—helping in the shop, keeping up with school, and still trying to be a kid in a close-knit community.  

When you think about why you later cared so much about girls getting the chance to learn, where did that concern take root—was there a particular moment, person, or situation in your community or school that made it clear to you?

I think it took root very early, just from what I saw around me. There were bright girls in my community who would start school with you, and then little by little they’d disappear—not because they weren’t clever, but because they were needed at home, or someone decided the boys’ schooling mattered more. I remember feeling it wasn’t fair, because I could see those girls had the same curiosity and ability, and yet their future was being narrowed so quickly.

I can’t point to one single dramatic moment, but I do remember a teacher who took time with me and a few other girls, and she spoke to us like our minds mattered. That stayed with me. Later, when I became a teacher myself, I kept thinking of those girls who never got the chance to finish, and it pushed me to speak up and to create spaces—like the reading clubs—where girls could feel supported and seen.

Thank you—what you describe, seeing girls quietly disappear from school and then having a teacher who made you feel your mind mattered, really explains the roots of your advocacy.

When you later began speaking up for girls and creating reading clubs, where was this happening—was it at the rural primary school where you first taught, or after you moved to the township, or both?

It was really both, but it grew much more after I moved to the township. In the rural primary school where I first taught, I did speak to families and encouraged them to keep the girls in school, and I tried to give the girls a bit of extra support in class when I could. But once I was in the township, there were more children, more parents moving in and out, and you could organize things a little more easily—so that’s where the reading club truly took shape and later spread to other schools. The township is where it became something bigger than just my own classroom.

I hear you—so you were already advocating in the rural school, but the township gave you the space and networks to organize something that could grow.

About when did you move to the township—what year or what stage of your life was it?

I moved to the township in 1976, when I got married. I was still a young teacher then—already qualified and teaching, but very much in those early years of building my life. It felt like a big change from the rural school, because everything was busier and growing quickly, and that’s when I started finding more people to work with and organise alongside.

Thank you—that pins it clearly to 1976, when you married, and it makes sense that the move opened up more connections.

Which township did you move to in 1976?
It was a township outside Durban. I usually just say “the township near Durban,” because after all these years people know the area I mean, but I don’t always mention the exact name in conversation.

I understand—thank you for explaining that.

When did you start using the more general phrase “the township near Durban” instead of the exact name, and is that mainly for privacy/safety, or just because it feels simpler these days?

I’d say I started doing that more in the last maybe 10 or 15 years. When I was younger I would name it without thinking, because it was just home and everyone around me knew it.

These days it’s mostly simplicity, especially when I’m talking to people who aren’t from Durban—they understand “near Durban” straight away. But yes, there’s also a small bit of privacy in it now that I’m older and you never know who is listening or where a conversation will travel. It’s not that I’m scared, it’s just being sensible.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in Poland, and my childhood was shaped by the war and the disruptions around it. There was a lot of uncertainty—things you’d normally take for granted, like school routines or having enough supplies, could change overnight. My family learned to make do and to be practical, and I think that’s where I got my habit of keeping calm and figuring out what can be done with what you have. I wasn’t a loud child; I mostly kept to myself, watched people, and learned quickly what was fair and what wasn’t. Even then, I wanted stability, and I carried that with me into adulthood.

Thank you—your description of staying calm, practical, and observant really comes through.  

Where in Poland did you grow up—what town or region was it, and what do you remember most vividly about that place when you were a child?

I grew up in Poland, but to be honest I don’t like to pin it down to a specific town in conversations like this—old habits from those times, I suppose. It was a more ordinary, working sort of place, not a grand city, and what I remember most vividly is how quickly the atmosphere could change: one week you had a routine, and the next you were adjusting again. I remember queues, empty shelves, and people trading or sharing what they had, quietly, without making a show of it. I also remember how the seasons felt very strong—cold winters especially—and how much life happened close to home, because you didn’t go far unless you had to. Mostly, I remember watching the adults’ faces and learning when to speak and when to stay silent.

That makes a lot of sense, and I respect your preference not to name the town.  

When you think back to those queues, shortages, and the “old habits” you mentioned, about how old were you then, and roughly what years or period was that?

I was a child and into my early teens when those habits really formed—so, roughly from about age six or seven up to maybe fourteen or fifteen. In terms of years, that would be the early 1940s through the later 1940s, and honestly some of it didn’t feel fully “normal” even right after the war ended. The queues and the empty shelves are especially tied in my mind to those war years and the immediate aftermath. It’s hard to put exact dates on it because, when you’re that age, you measure time more by what changed around you than by the calendar. But that general period is right.

Thank you—that’s very clear, and I understand what you mean about measuring time by changes rather than dates.  

When you say you learned “when to speak and when to stay silent,” who were the adults you were watching most (parents, relatives, neighbors, teachers), and what kinds of situations made silence feel necessary?

Mostly I watched my parents and other close family, and also the neighbors—people you’d see in the stairwell, in the courtyard, in the queue. Teachers too, when school was running as it should, but day to day it was the adults at home and around the building who taught you the most.

Silence felt necessary in public situations where you didn’t know who was listening, especially when adults were talking about shortages, money, or anything that sounded like a complaint. In the queues, you could feel tensions—someone might be angry, someone might be desperate—and a careless remark could turn into an argument very fast. And sometimes it was simply that the grown-ups were trying to protect the children, so you learned not to push with questions, and not to repeat things outside the home. You learned to read a face or a pause and understand, “Now is not the time.”

You painted that really vividly—the stairwell, courtyard, and queues, and how quickly a comment could escalate.  

In those day-to-day places around your building, was this in a city neighborhood, a small town, or something more rural—and what was the building/community like (for example, apartments with a shared courtyard, workers’ housing, close-knit neighbors)?

It was more like a small working town than anything rural, and not a grand city neighborhood either. We lived in an apartment building where people were on top of each other in the way you are in those places—shared stairwell, and a courtyard where you’d see the same faces every day. It felt like workers’ housing in the sense that most people had practical jobs and similar worries, and you could tell who was coming and going just by the sounds on the stairs.

The neighbors were close-knit, but not in a sentimental way—more that you depended on each other, so you paid attention. People shared small things when they could, or traded quietly, and they also watched their words because news and rumors traveled fast. The building itself wasn’t anything fancy, but it was the center of life: the queue started somewhere nearby, children played in the courtyard when it was possible, and adults talked in low voices in the stairwell.

Thank you—that gives a strong sense of the place and the kind of practical closeness people had.  

When you say the queue started somewhere nearby, what was it usually a queue for—and what did “traded quietly” typically involve in your building (what kinds of items or favors)?

Most often it was a queue for food—bread, flour, sometimes potatoes, things like that. Sometimes it was for whatever had “come in,” and you wouldn’t even know until you got closer what you were actually waiting for, but it was usually the basic supplies people needed to get through the week.

“Traded quietly” was small, practical things. A bit of sugar for a little soap, a few eggs if someone had access to them for a piece of cloth, or even just swapping something like buttons, thread, or a candle. And favors, too—someone holding your place in the line if you had to run back home, or sharing a little information like which shop might still have something left, but said softly so it didn’t turn into a scene. It wasn’t grand bartering, more like people helping each other survive without attracting attention or stirring up trouble.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a busy city, the kind of place where there was always noise—buses, market calls, neighbors talking across courtyards—so you learned to pay attention and still find your own space in the middle of it. As a child I was curious and always asking questions, the type that would take things apart just to see how they worked, which I think planted the seed for me later becoming a technician. We didn’t have a luxurious life, but it was lively and full of people looking out for one another, and that gave me a strong sense of community. I remember being drawn to radios early on—how a voice could travel into your home and make you feel connected to the wider world. Looking back, it was a straightforward upbringing, but it shaped my love for public service and communication in a very natural way.

That paints a vivid picture—busy, communal, and full of sound, with that early curiosity already showing.

Which city did you grow up in, and what was the neighborhood or area called where you lived?

I grew up in Lagos, and we lived around Surulere. It was a proper “everybody knows somebody” kind of place—busy streets, small shops, and neighbors who would call out to you like you were family. I can’t swear I remember every street name clearly now, but Surulere is the area I always think of as home from those early years.

Thank you—that helps, and it makes sense that the exact street names might blur over time.

Roughly what years or what ages were you living around Surulere—was that from your earliest childhood through your teen years, or did you move away at some point?

It was my earliest childhood right through my teen years, really. I was in Surulere from when I was very small—my first clear memories are there—and I stayed until I was about 18 or so. After secondary school, as I started looking for work and opportunities, that’s when I began spending more time outside the area and eventually moved on. I couldn’t give you an exact year off the top of my head, but it was definitely late teens when Surulere stopped being my everyday base.

That’s clear—Surulere was home right up through your late teens, and then things shifted after secondary school as you started chasing work and opportunities.

When Surulere stopped being your everyday base—around age 18—where did you go next, and about what year would you estimate that was?

After Surulere, I started spending most of my time on the Lagos Island side because that’s where more of the offices and station work opportunities seemed to be, and I was trying to get my foot in the door anywhere technical. I didn’t move to some far-off place immediately—I was still very much in Lagos—but my “everyday base” shifted because I was commuting and sometimes staying with a relative or a friend when work ran late. 

If I’m estimating the year, it would’ve been mid-to-late 1970s, around 1976 or 1977, give or take. I’m not completely certain on the exact year, but it was right in that period just before I properly joined the radio station in 1978.

Thank you—that timeline helps a lot, especially placing it just before you joined the station in 1978.

Where was the radio station you properly joined in 1978 located—what was the name of the station, and what part of Lagos was it in?

It was on the Lagos Island side, around the Marina/Broad Street area. The truth is, after all these years I’m a bit cautious about naming the exact station on record, but it was one of the main broadcast houses in that part of town, and that’s where I first properly got taken on as a technician in 1978. Most days, just getting into that building felt like I’d finally stepped into the world I’d been chasing.

I understand, and thank you for sharing the area—that’s plenty without naming the station.

How did you first get taken on as a technician there in 1978—was it through an application, a recommendation, some kind of training, or another opportunity?

It was a bit of a mix, but mainly a recommendation opened the door. I’d been hanging around that Lagos Island area looking for any technical work I could find, and I’d done small jobs—fixing and checking equipment—so people could see I was serious and could handle my hands. Someone who already had a foot in the broadcast house put my name forward and told them, “This young man is steady, bring him in.” After that, I still had to show what I could do, so it started almost like a trial—long hours, learning their routines, and being watched closely. Once they were satisfied I was reliable and careful with the equipment, they took me on properly as a technician.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in Vietnam, and my childhood was pretty simple and close to family. We didn’t have much, so everyone helped out, and you learned to be practical early—how to make things last, how to fix and mend instead of replacing. I was quiet as a kid, more the type to listen and watch, and I liked anything with my hands, like sewing and small crafts. Even then, I remember the feeling of community—neighbors coming by, sharing food, helping each other during hard times. It wasn’t always easy, but it gave me a strong sense of responsibility and how important family is.

Thank you for sharing that—it really paints a clear picture of a practical, close-knit childhood.  

Which part of Vietnam did you grow up in (city or province), and about what years was that?

I grew up in southern Vietnam, around the Saigon area—what people now call Ho Chi Minh City. I was born in the mid-1950s, so my childhood would’ve been mostly in the late 1950s through the 1960s, and into the early 1970s before I left. I don’t always remember the exact years for everything, but that’s the general time.

That helps a lot—thank you for placing it around Saigon in the late 1950s through early 1970s.  

When you say you left in the early 1970s, can you tell me what year (even an estimate) you left Saigon, and where you went at that time?

It’s a little blurry for me to pin down an exact year, but I believe it was around 1973 or 1974 when I left Saigon. At that time I didn’t go straight to the country I eventually immigrated to—I first went to stay with relatives for a while, just outside the city, to get through that transition. The big move abroad came later, in 1979, when I finally migrated and started working in garment manufacturing.

Thank you—that timeline makes sense: leaving Saigon around 1973–1974, staying with relatives near the city, then migrating in 1979.  

Where did you migrate to in 1979—the country where you began working in garment manufacturing?

I migrated to the United States in 1979. That’s where I first started working in garment manufacturing, doing factory shifts and trying to get my footing in a new place. It was a big change, but I was focused on working and learning the language little by little.

Thank you—so in 1979 you came to the United States and began factory shifts in garment manufacturing while learning the language.  

In the U.S., what city or area did you live in when you first arrived and started that garment factory job?

When I first arrived in 1979, I was in the Los Angeles area. I don’t always remember every exact neighborhood name now, but it was around where there were a lot of garment factories and other immigrants, so it felt like the right place to find work quickly. That’s where I started those factory shifts in garment manufacturing.

Thank you—that’s very helpful. In Los Angeles, do you remember any specific neighborhood name, nearby street, landmark, or even the bus line you took that might help place where that first factory job was located?

I wish I could give you an exact street, but I honestly don’t remember the name now. I just remember it being near downtown, in that area where there were many garment shops and warehouses, and a lot of other immigrant workers. I took the bus, but I can’t recall the line number—more that it was a regular city bus route that stopped along bigger streets and I’d walk the last few blocks. What sticks in my mind is the feeling of being in a busy commercial area with a lot of sewing factories close together, not a quiet residential neighborhood.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a little coastal town, the kind of place where the weather is always part of the conversation and everyone seems to know whose boat is whose. As a child I was forever wandering between the shoreline and the library, depending on whether I wanted salt air or a quiet corner. Life felt simpler then—busy in its own way, but with a strong rhythm to it, tied to seasons and community events. I was a curious, bookish girl, and I liked listening to older people tell stories about the town, which I suppose explains how I ended up loving local history later on. We didn’t have much in the way of fancy entertainment, but I never felt short on things to do if I had a book and a bit of imagination.
That picture of moving between the shoreline and the library really comes through. What was the name of the coastal town where you grew up?

It was a small coastal town, but to be honest I tend to just call it that now—“my little coastal town”—because I don’t always like to pin it down by name in interviews. What matters most to me is the feel of it: the harbor talk, the wind, and everyone keeping half an eye on the tides. If you’re asking because you’re trying to place the kind of community it was, it was very much a working waterfront sort of place.

That makes complete sense, and thank you for explaining what feels comfortable to share. When you think of that working waterfront community, what kinds of work did your family or close neighbors do around the harbor, and what did a typical day look like for you as a child?

Mostly it was fishing and all the supporting jobs that go with it. A few of our close neighbors were out on boats—lobster pots, nets, that sort of thing—and others worked more onshore, mending gear, doing small repairs, or working at places that iced and packed the catch. There were also people who seemed to do a bit of everything depending on the season, and you could always tell what time of day it was by the way the harbor sounded.

For me, a typical day was school, chores, and then I’d roam if I could—down to the water to watch the boats come in, or up to the library to disappear into a book. You learned to pay attention without being told: the sky, the wind, whether the tide was in or out, because it shaped everyone’s plans. And if I found an older person willing to talk, I’d perch nearby and listen—half for the stories, half because that’s just what you did in a town where everybody’s life touched the harbor.

I love how you describe “telling the time” by the harbor sounds, and how observant you became about sky, wind, and tides. What were your regular chores at home, and was there one chore you especially liked—or really disliked?

We weren’t a fancy household, so my chores were the plain, everyday sort: helping with the washing up, drying dishes, keeping things swept, and doing little errands when someone needed something picked up. I also did my share of peeling potatoes, setting the table, and hanging laundry—on a windy day it would snap like a sail, which felt very “on brand” for our town.

I actually didn’t mind the laundry much, especially if I could do it and keep an eye on the sky at the same time. The chore I disliked most was scrubbing floors—there’s something about being on your hands and knees that makes even a cheerful child feel put-upon. And if I’m being honest, anything that kept me from slipping off to the library felt like the worst chore in the world at the time.

You’ve painted that so clearly—laundry snapping like a sail, and scrubbing floors feeling especially unfair when the library was calling. About how old were you when you were doing those chores regularly?

I started having regular chores pretty young—around seven or eight, I’d say—little things at first, like drying dishes or running small errands. By the time I was ten or eleven, I was doing them as a matter of course, not just “helping out,” and there was more expected of me. The floor-scrubbing showed up somewhere in that later stretch, and I remember thinking it was an outrage every single time. Of course, it all blurs a bit now, but that’s about the age range.

Thank you—that age range helps, and your “outrage” at floor-scrubbing made me smile. When you were scrubbing those floors around ten or eleven, where was it—what was the home like (for example, a small house near the harbor, an apartment, something older), and what room(s) were you usually scrubbing?

It was a small, older house—nothing grand—set in among other working families, not right on the wharf but close enough that you always felt the harbor in the air. I can’t picture the exact paint colors anymore, but I remember it as sturdy and a bit worn in the way those houses get when everyone’s coming in and out with muddy shoes and salt on their coats.

The floors I was usually scrubbing were the kitchen and the entryway—where everything landed first. If there was company or some kind of church-do coming up, then it might extend into the front room, but the kitchen was the main battleground. That was where the real life happened, and also where the mess happened, so that’s where I ended up on my hands and knees, feeling terribly wronged.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a fairly modest neighborhood, the kind of place where you knew your neighbors and people looked out for each other. As a child, life felt simple but structured—school, helping at home, and spending a lot of time outdoors. I was always the sort of kid who liked to understand how things worked, so I’d tinker with small repairs or get curious about machines and tools whenever I had the chance. We didn’t have much in the way of luxuries, but there was a strong emphasis on education and doing things properly. Looking back, that mix of practical life and steady routine probably shaped the calm, methodical way I approached work later on.

That sounds like a close-knit, steady upbringing, and I hear how that practical routine and curiosity about tools stayed with you. Where exactly was this neighborhood—what city or region and country did you grow up in?

I grew up in Alexandria, Egypt, in a modest neighborhood where everyone more or less knew each other. It wasn’t fancy, but it felt solid—people kept an eye out, and you learned early to be respectful and do things properly. Being in Alexandria also meant you saw a mix of practical trades and everyday problem-solving around you, which probably fed that curiosity I had about how things worked.

Thank you—that really helps place it. About what years was this, roughly (or how old were you), when you were growing up in that Alexandria neighborhood and first becoming curious about how things worked?

It would’ve been in the late 1950s into the 1960s, roughly. I was probably around six or seven when I first started really paying attention to how things worked—asking questions, taking an interest in tools, that sort of thing. By the time I was a teenager, it was more deliberate: I’d try small fixes around the house and watch the tradesmen and mechanics when I could. I can’t pin every year exactly, but that’s the general period.
When you were a teenager doing those small fixes and watching tradesmen, where was this happening—still in Alexandria in the same neighborhood, or somewhere else?

It was still in Alexandria, and mostly in the same neighborhood. We didn’t move around much when I was growing up, so the “small fixes” were usually at home or for a neighbor—simple things you could do with basic tools. And the tradesmen I watched were the local people working nearby, the kind you’d see in the street or in small workshops, so it was all very close to where I lived. I might have gone across town occasionally with family, but the everyday learning and observing was really from that same area.
When you went across town with your family as a child, about how old were you, and who would take you and for what kinds of occasions?

I was maybe eight or nine the first times I really remember going across town, and then a bit more often as I got into my early teens. Usually it was with my father, and sometimes my mother would come along depending on what it was. It was mostly practical occasions—visiting relatives, going to a bigger market for something you couldn’t easily find near our neighborhood, or handling some errand that needed an office or a shop in a different part of the city. Every now and then it was for something connected to school, like paperwork or buying supplies, but it wasn’t frequent—more occasional trips than a regular routine.
In your early teens, when those trips became a bit more frequent, about how often would you say they happened—was it weekly, monthly, or just a few times a year?

In my early teens I’d say it was more like monthly, maybe—certainly not every week. Some months there might be two trips if there were errands piling up or a family visit, and other times you could go a couple of months without going. So overall, I’d put it somewhere between monthly and a few times a year, but closer to monthly than weekly.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a fairly ordinary neighborhood, the kind where everyone knew each other and you could run errands for your parents without it feeling like a big deal. As a child I was curious and a bit perfectionistic—I liked making things with my hands and I’d get absorbed in details, whether it was helping in the kitchen or doing little projects at home. Food was a big part of family life, not in a fancy way, just in the sense that meals and sweets marked holidays and weekends. I remember being drawn to the calm of baking—measuring, watching something change in the oven, trying again if it didn’t come out right. I wasn’t the loudest kid, but I was cheerful, and once I set my mind on something I tended to stick with it.

That sounds like a warm, close-knit place to grow up, and I can really hear how the calm, repeatable steps of baking appealed to you.  

Where was this neighborhood—what city or region (and country) did you grow up in?

I grew up in Bulgaria, in the Sofia area. It wasn’t anything glamorous—just a very typical neighborhood where people recognized you on the street and neighbors would keep an eye out for each other. Even as a kid, that kind of environment made it feel normal to be out and about doing small errands, and home life really revolved around the kitchen and family meals.

Thank you—that helps me place it: Bulgaria, around Sofia, with that familiar neighborhood feel.  

When you think back to your home life there, who was in your household as you were growing up (parents, siblings, grandparents), and who was most influential in the kitchen?

In our home it was mostly my parents, my sister, and me. My grandparents weren’t living with us full-time, but they were around enough—visiting, coming for holidays—that they still felt like part of the rhythm of the house. In the kitchen, my mother was the center of everything day to day; she was the one cooking the regular meals and keeping things running. But my grandmother was the one who really influenced me when it came to baking and sweets—she had that patient, “do it properly” way about her, and she didn’t mind if you tried again until it came out right. Between the two of them, I learned both the practical side of feeding people and the careful, almost ritual side of making pastries.

I appreciate how clearly you describe the different roles—your mother grounding the everyday meals, and your grandmother bringing that patient “do it properly” pastry tradition.  

Do you remember a specific pastry or sweet your grandmother taught you to make, and what you were doing step-by-step alongside her?

Yes—I remember her teaching me how to make banitsa, the kind with thin layers and a simple cheese filling. She’d start by laying out the sheets and covering them with a clean cloth so they didn’t dry out, and my job was to be careful with my hands and not tear them. We’d mix the filling together—she’d have me crumble the cheese finely and stir it with eggs and a bit of yogurt, and she’d keep reminding me to mix evenly so you didn’t get one bite that was all salt and the next that was plain. Then she’d show me how to brush each layer with melted butter or oil, not soaking it, just enough so it baked up tender and crisp, and we’d build it slowly in the pan. Before it went into the oven, she’d have me score the top lightly so it was easier to portion later, and then we’d watch it bake until it was deeply golden and you could smell that toasty, buttery edge. If it came out a little pale or soggy once, she didn’t scold—she’d just say, “Next time, hotter oven, and don’t rush the layering,” like it was the most normal thing in the world to try again.

That’s such a vivid memory—the cloth over the sheets, the careful brushing, scoring the top, and her calm “try again” approach.  

Where were you when she taught you to make banitsa—was it in your family home in the Sofia area, at your grandmother’s place, or somewhere else?

It was mostly in our family home in the Sofia area. My grandmother would come over, especially around holidays or weekends when everyone was already thinking about food, and we’d take over the kitchen table together. Sometimes I’d watch her do it at her place too, but the lesson I remember best—the cloth over the sheets and all that—was in our kitchen at home, with my mother coming in and out and the whole house smelling like it was turning into something.

That home-kitchen scene really comes alive—your grandmother at the table, your mother moving in and out, and the smell filling the house.  

About how old were you during those banitsa lessons at home, and roughly how often did you and your grandmother bake together (every weekend, mainly holidays, a few times a year)?

I was probably around nine or ten when she first started really letting me help properly—old enough that she trusted my hands not to rush and tear the sheets. We didn’t do it every single weekend, but it was fairly regular: more often around holidays, and then some weekends when she came by and we had time. If I had to put it simply, I’d say a few times a month during the colder seasons, and then less in the summer. It felt frequent because it was tied to family gatherings, so it stood out in my memory.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up on family land in the countryside, around many relatives, so home never felt like just one small household. As a child, life was simple and very practical—chores, helping elders, and learning to do things properly the first time because there wasn’t always money or time to waste. We walked a lot, and you learned the seasons by what people were planting, harvesting, or repairing. There was also a strong sense of respect—if an older person spoke, you listened, and if a task was given, you finished it. Looking back, it wasn’t easy, but it made me steady, and it taught me patience early.
That sounds like a busy, close-knit childhood with a lot of responsibility and learning. Which part of the countryside was your family land in—what region or nearest town do you remember?

It was in the Rift Valley area, not far from Eldoret. I remember it more by the feel of the place than by exact boundaries—open land, farms spread out, and neighbors who were also family in one way or another. In those days we didn’t always speak in terms of “regions” the way people do now; you just named the nearest town or the trading center. Eldoret is the one I can say clearly.

Thank you—that really helps paint the picture, and I understand what you mean about naming places by the nearest center. Besides Eldoret, what was the nearest trading center or smaller town you and your family would go to most often?

Besides Eldoret, the one we would mention most often was Turbo. It was closer for small things—salt, paraffin, a few household items—so you didn’t always need to go all the way into Eldoret. My memory of the exact names is not perfect now, but Turbo is the trading center that comes to mind when I think of where we went regularly.

I appreciate that—and Turbo is a helpful detail. When was it that you were making those regular trips to Turbo for things like salt and paraffin—about how old were you, or what stage of life was it (childhood, teenage years, after you were married)?

That was mostly when I was still young, before I was married—more like my teenage years and into early adulthood. In childhood I went sometimes, but usually with older people; as I got bigger and stronger, I could be sent or go along more regularly for those small necessities. After I married, we still used Turbo for quick things, but by then I was already moving around more for work and you plan your buying differently. So when I think of those “regular trips” for salt and paraffin, it’s that period before marriage, when I was helping the household more seriously.
When you say that after you married you were “moving around more for work,” what kind of work was that, and where did it usually take you?

After I married, I started getting more serious transport work—driving long-distance deliveries. Before that I was mostly around home and doing what the household needed, but once I took that kind of job I was on the road for days at a time.

The work would take me out from our area near Eldoret to different trading centers and towns along the main roads, depending on what was being carried and where it was needed. I can’t recall every place name clearly now, but it was that pattern of moving between market towns and depots, not staying in one place. That’s what I meant—work that made you plan your buying ahead because you wouldn’t be home every day.
About what year was that—when you got married and then began doing those long-distance driving jobs that kept you on the road for days at a time?

I got married around 1963, and it was not long after that—within a few years—that I began doing the long-distance driving more seriously, the kind that kept me on the road for days. I may not remember the exact year I started those longer runs, but it was in the early to mid-1960s, when I had to shift from being mostly at home to being away for work.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a family of artisans, in a place where making things by hand was just part of everyday life. As a child, I was always surrounded by materials and tools, and I learned early to pay attention to how things were put together—what made something sturdy, what made it comfortable, what made it beautiful. It wasn’t a fancy childhood, but it was rich in a different way: lots of practical creativity, lots of pride in doing work properly. I remember being curious and a bit stubborn, always wanting to understand why spaces felt welcoming or not. Looking back, it makes sense that I eventually found my way into architecture.
That sounds like a childhood full of hands-on learning and careful attention to craft. Where did you grow up—what city or region was it, and what country?

I grew up in Iran, in Tehran. It’s a big, busy place, but our world felt very centered around the home and the workshop spaces—people always fixing, building, sewing, carving, that kind of thing. Even in a city like that, you notice very quickly which streets and courtyards feel human and comfortable, and which ones feel harsh or unwelcoming.

Tehran sounds like it gave you both that close, workshop-centered world and a very sharp eye for how public spaces feel. In Tehran, can you describe a specific street, courtyard, or neighborhood that felt especially welcoming to you as a child—and what about it made it feel that way?

One that stays with me is the older, more traditional lanes near the bazaar area—those narrower streets where the buildings and shopfronts almost “hold” you as you walk. I can’t swear I remember the exact name of a lane now, but I remember the feeling very clearly. There was shade for much of the day, little pauses in the street where you could step aside without being pushed along, and courtyards tucked behind doors that made the city feel less overwhelming.

What made it welcoming was how human-scaled it was: you could walk close to the walls, exchange greetings, smell food or spices, hear the sounds of work, and feel like you belonged there even as a child. It wasn’t designed as some grand statement—it was comfortable, practical, and softened by everyday life, and that combination stayed with me.

You described that sense of being “held” by those lanes so vividly—shade, pauses to step aside, and hidden courtyards making the city feel manageable. About what age were you when you were spending time in those bazaar-area lanes most often?

I’d say I was most often there around late childhood into my early teens, maybe from about nine or ten up to fourteen or so. That’s when I was old enough to be sent along on errands and to wander a little more independently, but still young enough that the city could feel overwhelming if a place wasn’t kind to you. I don’t remember an exact year, but that age range feels right in my body when I think of those walks.

When you were around 9 to 14 and being sent on errands, where exactly in Tehran were those lanes—do you remember which part of the bazaar area (or any nearby landmark, mosque, square, or major street) that helps place it?

I wish I could give you an exact lane name, but I really can’t swear to it anymore. In my mind it was around the Grand Bazaar area, the older part of the city—those covered and semi-covered passages where you move between little courtyards and workshops. I remember it being not far from the Golestan Palace side of things, and the feeling of coming in from the bigger streets into those tighter lanes, but the specific mosque or square name is fuzzy for me now. What I’m most sure of is the texture of it: the shaded passages, the turnings, and how quickly the noise and scale changed once you were inside.

That’s very clear—especially the “coming in from the bigger streets into those tighter lanes” and how the noise and scale changed. When you were doing those errands in the bazaar area, what kinds of errands were you usually sent on, and who were you doing them for?

Mostly it was practical things for the household and for my family’s work—small purchases and deliveries that kept everything moving. I’d be sent to pick up notions for sewing, or simple hardware like nails, hooks, bits of wire, sometimes glue or polishing materials, depending on what someone was repairing that week. Often it was for my mother or an older relative at home, and sometimes for a family friend or one of the craftspeople we knew through the workshop circle—“take this, bring that back, ask the price, see if it’s ready.”  

I also remember being sent for spices or tea, the kinds of things you could carry easily and that gave you an excuse to walk those passages and greet people. Nothing dramatic—just everyday errands—but they taught me how a neighborhood works when it’s made of many small, dependable connections.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up near the harbor, so the sea and the sound of work were always around—boats coming in, men shouting, metal clanging. As a kid, life felt busy but close-knit; neighbors knew each other, and you learned early to mind your manners and help out when someone needed a hand. I remember being fascinated by the shipyards even before I worked there, watching people do skilled jobs and thinking it was something to be proud of. We didn’t have a lot of luxuries, but there was a strong sense of routine—school, chores, and family meals—and that steadiness shaped me. Being around that kind of hardworking environment made me careful and serious about doing things the right way.
That sounds like a vivid, hardworking childhood—those harbor sounds really stay with you. Which harbor town or city was this, and what were your schooldays, chores, and family meals like there?

It was in Greece, in the Piraeus area near the port of Athens. School was pretty straightforward and strict—teachers expected you to behave, do your work, and not make excuses, and I was the kind of kid who tried to keep his head down and do things properly. After school I’d help however I could, mostly simple chores—running errands, carrying things for the house, sometimes helping a neighbor if they needed an extra set of hands. And our family meals were the anchor of the day: nothing fancy, but everyone came together, you ate what was on the table, and you listened more than you talked. That routine—and being surrounded by working people—made me take pride in doing a job carefully.

Thank you—Piraeus near the port paints a clear picture, and those family meals sound like they really set your values. Who was at the table with you in those years—your parents, siblings, or other relatives—and what were they like?

Most nights it was my mother and father, and I had siblings at the table too. We also had an uncle around quite a bit—one of those family setups where relatives were never far, and if someone was on hard times or needed help, they’d be there for a while. My father was a quiet, working man—tired at the end of the day, not big on speeches, but you could feel what he expected just by the way he carried himself. My mother held the whole thing together, practical and warm, the kind of woman who could make a simple meal feel like it mattered. And the kids—me included—we learned to wait our turn, listen, and not waste food, because that table wasn’t just about eating, it was about respect.
You’ve described that table as a place where respect was taught, not just shared food. When your uncle stayed with you, what led him to come and go like that, and what do you remember most about him?

In those days it wasn’t unusual—if a man hit a rough patch with work, or there was some trouble at home, he’d come stay with family until he got back on his feet. My uncle was that kind of case: he’d be around for a stretch, then he’d find steady work again or things would settle, and he’d move on. I don’t remember every detail of why each time, honestly—it was talked about quietly, not made into a big story for the kids.

What I remember most about him was how he carried himself at the table. He wasn’t loud, but he had a presence—always watched, listened, and when he did speak it was usually something practical, like reminding us to be respectful, finish what’s on the plate, help your mother, that kind of thing. He’d also bring little bits of news from the port—who got hired, who got injured, what ship came in—so he connected the house to that working world outside. And even when he was the one needing help, he’d still find a way to be useful, fixing something small around the house or lending a hand, so you learned that taking help didn’t mean you stopped having pride.

That’s very clear—and I appreciate how you explain the way it was handled quietly, with dignity. When your uncle came to stay, where was your family living at that time in Piraeus (the neighborhood or area, if you remember), and about how old were you during those stretches?

We were in the Piraeus area proper, not far from the port—close enough that you could walk down and feel that whole harbor life around you. I wish I could give you the exact neighborhood name with confidence, but after all these years I’m not sure I’d say the right one, so I don’t want to make something up.

As for my age, I was mostly a schoolboy when he was coming and going—roughly late childhood into my early teens. By the time I was older and starting to think seriously about work, it happened less, or at least it didn’t feel like such a big thing because I understood more.

That makes sense—and it’s completely fine not to force a neighborhood name. When you were older and starting to think seriously about work, where were you living then—still in the same part of Piraeus near the port, or had your family moved somewhere else?

I was still in the same general part of Piraeus, near the port. We didn’t have the kind of life where people were moving around a lot—family and work were there, and you stayed close to what you knew. As I got into my later teens and started thinking about a trade, I was spending more time around the yards and the waterfront anyway, so it made sense to be based there. If there were any small changes, it was more like shifting to a different street or building nearby, not leaving the area.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a small place above my parents’ shop, so home and work were always mixed together. As a child, I learned to be helpful early—there was always something to do, whether it was watching the counter for a moment, running small errands, or helping my mother with cooking. My parents also taught me simple bookkeeping, just little sums and keeping track of what came in and what went out, and I think that stayed with me. It wasn’t a fancy childhood, but it felt steady, and we looked after each other. I remember it as busy, practical, and close-knit.
That sounds like a steady, close-knit home with a lot of responsibility woven into everyday life. Where was your parents’ shop located—what town or city (and country) did you grow up in?

I grew up in Hong Kong, in one of the older, busier urban districts where shops were packed close together and people lived right above their work. I don’t always remember the exact street name anymore, but it was very much that crowded city feeling—markets nearby, neighbors always passing through, and noise from early morning to late at night. Hong Kong is really what shaped me, because you learned to be practical and to keep going.

Thank you—that crowded, market-filled Hong Kong neighborhood comes through clearly. About what years were you growing up there, or roughly how old were you when you were living above the shop in that busy district?

I was born around 1940, so my growing-up years in that neighborhood would’ve been the 1940s into the early 1950s. I lived above the shop all through childhood and into my teens, until I started working and spending more time out in the world. The clearest memories are from when I was maybe six or seven onward, because that’s when I was trusted to run little errands and help watch the counter. Exact years blur a bit now, but it was definitely before things felt more modern and spread out.

That helps a lot—especially the memory of being trusted with errands around six or seven. When you started working and spending more time out in the world, where was that—was it still in the same Hong Kong district, or did you travel to a different area for your first job?

It was still in Hong Kong, but not right on the same street as our shop. I started going to a different area by tram and bus, so it felt like I was “out in the world,” even though it wasn’t far in distance. I honestly can’t recall the exact district name now, but it was more of a commercial area with offices and small companies, not the same crowded row of neighborhood shops I grew up around. In those days you didn’t have to go very far for it to feel like a big change—you were just on your own schedule, meeting different kinds of people.

I understand—just taking the tram and bus to a more office-and-company area could feel like a big step. About how old were you when you first began making those trips to that different commercial area on your own?

I was about sixteen, maybe seventeen, the first time I started making those trips on my own regularly. It’s a little blurry exactly when it began, but it was in my later teen years, when I was starting to work and had my own schedule. Before that, my world was mostly just the shop, the market, and nearby streets. Taking the tram and bus by myself made me feel much more grown up, even if it wasn’t so far.

Thank you—that sense of independence at sixteen or seventeen is very vivid. What were you usually going to in that commercial area when you took the tram and bus—was it a specific workplace, a training or bookkeeping job, errands for the shop, or something else?

Most of the time I was going to work. It wasn’t a big, famous place—more like small offices and companies where they needed someone young to help with numbers and paperwork, and I was learning as I went. I did a bit of everything at first: running documents, helping with simple accounts, adding up figures, that kind of thing, and it slowly became more “bookkeeping” as I got steadier.

Sometimes, if my parents needed something, I would also pick up a few supplies or take care of a small errand on the way back, but the main reason for those trips was that first job and getting used to having my own schedule. It felt very different from the shop, because in an office you had to be quiet and careful, and you were dealing with people who weren’t your neighbors.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a pretty ordinary working-class neighborhood in the city, the kind of place where you knew most of the folks on your street and you learned early to look out for each other. As a kid, I was outdoors a lot—riding my bike, playing pickup games, getting into the usual harmless trouble and then trying not to bring it home on my clothes. My parents were steady, practical people, and there was a strong expectation that you did what you said you’d do and you helped when someone needed it. Money wasn’t lavish, but we had what we needed, and the house ran on routine and responsibility. Looking back, that mix of community and no-nonsense values probably shaped me more than anything.
That sounds like a close-knit, practical kind of upbringing. Which city and neighborhood was it, and about what years were you growing up there?

It was in the city, but to be honest I tend to just say “a working-class neighborhood on the east side” rather than name the exact spot—old habit from the job, I suppose. Timewise, I was a kid there through the late ’50s into the ’60s, and I was still around the same area as a teenager before work and life pulled me farther out. It wasn’t anything fancy, but it was the kind of place where people watched out for one another, and that stuck with me.

I understand—and thank you for sharing the timeframe. When you say you were still around the same area as a teenager before work and life pulled you farther out, about how old were you when you started moving away from that neighborhood, and what was the first work or life change that prompted it?

I’d say I was around 18 or 19 when I really started drifting away from that neighborhood day-to-day. The first big change was work—once I finished school and started working full-time, I wasn’t just knocking around the block with my friends anymore, and my days were built around getting to a job and being on time, not being home for dinner. Then, not long after, I joined the fire service in my early twenties, and that really did it—between the training and the shifts, you end up spending more time at the station or commuting than you do on the old stoop. I didn’t move far at first, but it stopped being the center of my world.

That makes a lot of sense—full-time work and then the fire service would really reshape your days. When you say you didn’t move far at first, where did you move to (even just the general area), and about what year was that?

I didn’t go far at all at first—still on the east side, just a couple neighborhoods over, closer to where I could get to work and, later on, to the station without it being a whole production. I’m a little fuzzy on the exact year, but it would’ve been around when I was about 20 or so, so early ’70s—maybe ’73 or thereabouts. It wasn’t some big “fresh start” move, more like trading a family place for something small and practical so I could keep up with the hours.

When you say you were trading “a family place” for something small and practical, what was that family place—were you living with your parents, or in another shared family home—and what kind of place did you move into?

The “family place” was my parents’ place—same house I grew up in. I wasn’t a kid anymore, obviously, but I was still under their roof, and it was the kind of setup where you had your responsibilities and you didn’t come and go like you owned the place.

What I moved into was nothing glamorous: a small rental, basically a simple little apartment a couple neighborhoods over on the east side. One bedroom, place to sleep, a small kitchen—more about being practical and closer to where I needed to be than putting down some big roots right then.

Thanks—that paints it clearly. About how long did you stay in that one-bedroom rental, and what were your days and nights like then (work hours, getting to the station, what you did on your time off)?

I was in that one-bedroom rental for a couple of years, give or take—long enough to get my feet under me and get settled into the rhythm of working and then getting into the fire service. At first it was straight full-time work, early mornings, trying to be dependable and show up on time, and I picked the place mostly so the commute wouldn’t chew up half my day.

Once I got on the job, the days and nights were built around the station schedule—long shifts, odd hours, and you learned pretty quickly to sleep when you could and not waste energy running all over town. Getting to the station was basically part of the routine: up, coffee, out the door, and you made sure you were squared away before you walked in. On time off, I kept it simple—caught up on sleep, did chores, maybe saw family, and tried to have a normal meal once in a while. I wasn’t out looking for excitement; the work gave you plenty of that.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]
