transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in Romania, in a fairly ordinary, working family environment where you learned early to be practical and not waste things. As a child I was a bit serious and observant—I liked routine, and I remember being the type to line things up, keep my school materials neat, and feel proud when I did something “correctly.” School was important in our house, so I spent a lot of time studying, especially the sciences, because they made sense to me and felt dependable. I also remember having a quiet creative side, but it wasn’t something I treated as a “real” pursuit back then—more like something you did when the work was done. Overall it was a steady childhood, with a lot of emphasis on discipline and doing your part.

Thank you—that gives a very clear sense of the discipline and steadiness you grew up with.  

When you think back to that “quiet creative side” in childhood, where did that take place and what kinds of creative things did you do when the work was done?

Mostly it took place at home, in our apartment, at the kitchen table once homework was finished and everything was put away. I didn’t have a separate “art space” or anything like that—more like I’d pull out a notebook or scraps of paper and do quiet things while the adults were busy. I drew a lot, usually simple sketches of what I saw around me—little scenes, objects, sometimes bits of nature from memory if I’d been outside. I also liked arranging things, in a way that felt creative to me—making things look orderly and balanced, even if it was just how I set up my school supplies or helped tidy. It was never talked about as a talent or something to develop, just a pleasant way to spend time when the real responsibilities were done.

I hear you—quiet, private creativity at the kitchen table, more about calm and balance than “being an artist.”

When you were doing that at the kitchen table, about how old were you (or what school years), and who were the adults around you in the apartment?

That was mostly when I was in primary school and the early part of secondary school—so, roughly from maybe 8 or 9 up to around 13 or 14. It was that age where you’re old enough to be left to your homework and your own little routines, but you’re still very much under the rhythm of the household.

The adults around were mainly my parents. My mother was usually moving between the kitchen and whatever needed doing—cooking, tidying, laundry—always busy with something practical. My father was often either working late or home but tired, reading or doing quiet things of his own. Sometimes there were other relatives in and out, but day to day it was really my parents, and I’d be at the table finishing my work and then slipping into drawing for a little while.

That household rhythm comes through clearly—your mother constantly keeping things running, and your father coming in late or keeping quiet.

Which part of Romania was this apartment in (the city or town), and if you’re comfortable saying, what kind of work did your father do that kept him working late?

It was in Bucharest, in a pretty typical apartment block neighborhood—not anything picturesque, just very normal city life.

My father worked in an industrial, technical kind of job—engineering-related—and his hours could run long, especially when there were deadlines or problems to fix. I don’t remember all the exact details of his title now, but it was the sort of work where staying late wasn’t unusual, and when he got home he mostly wanted quiet.

Thank you—that helps place it: Bucharest, and your father in an engineering-related industrial role with long hours.

Do you remember where he worked—what kind of place it was (for example a factory, a plant, a workshop, a state enterprise), or the name of the workplace if it comes to you?

I don’t really remember the exact name of the workplace anymore, and I’m not sure I ever knew it clearly as a child. In my mind it was a state enterprise kind of environment—industrial, more like a plant or large factory setup than a small workshop. I mainly remember it through the rhythm it created: long days, sometimes coming home late, and that sense that if something broke or a deadline was pressing, he just had to stay. If you asked me to name it now, I’d be guessing, so I’d rather not put a wrong name to it.

That makes a lot of sense—and it’s helpful even without a name: the “state enterprise” feel and, especially, the rhythm it imposed on the household.

When your father did come home late and wanted quiet, how did that shape the evening at home for you—were there particular rules, routines, or feelings you remember around those nights?

Those nights got very quiet, almost like the apartment “shrunk” a little. My mother would lower her voice and move around more carefully, and I learned to do the same—finish what I was doing, put things away neatly, not leave anything out that would make noise or look messy. There wasn’t some dramatic rule written on the wall, but you understood: don’t ask for much, don’t start an argument, and don’t make extra work.

I remember a feeling of waiting, too—waiting to see what mood he came in with. If he was simply tired, it was mostly calm, and he’d sit with his book or just eat quietly. If he’d had a hard day, you could feel the tension and you tried to be “good,” almost invisible, which is probably why my drawing and little orderly routines suited me so well.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a small rural place where everyone knew your name and, more importantly, knew your family. Life was simple and pretty grounded in the land—early mornings, helping out where you were needed, and learning to listen to the older ones. As a child I spent a lot of time outdoors, and you learned quickly to be careful and to respect what feeds you, whether that was the bush, the river, or the gardens. We didn’t have much in the way of luxuries, but there was a strong sense of community—people shared what they had, and you were taught to carry your weight. Looking back, that upbringing is what gave me my patience and the habit of thinking about the group, not just myself.

That sounds like a close, grounded upbringing, with a lot of learning from the land and from elders.  

When you think back to that time, what was the name of the place or area where you grew up (and what country was it in)?

I grew up in rural Aotearoa New Zealand, up in the North Island. The area was around the Bay of Plenty, not in a big town—more the kind of place where the bush and the river were part of your daily life. I don’t always name the exact little settlement these days because it’s changed so much over the years, but that’s the part of the country that feels like “home” in my bones.

Thank you—Bay of Plenty in the North Island gives a clear picture, and I understand not wanting to name the exact settlement.  

About what years are we talking for those childhood days by the bush and river—roughly when were you born, or what decade were you a child in?

I was born in the early 1930s, so my childhood would’ve been through the 1930s and into the early 1940s. Those were the years I remember most clearly—being out by the river and in the bush, and doing whatever jobs needed doing around home. Time goes quickly when I think about it now, but that’s the decade range.

That helps a lot—1930s into the early 1940s. When you picture an ordinary day as a child back then, what would you typically be doing from morning to night—especially the kinds of things you did by the river or in the bush?

Most days started early, with someone up and moving before the sun was properly up. You’d have a bit of food, then it was straight into whatever jobs were on—hauling water, feeding animals if we had them, weeding the gardens, chopping kindling, that sort of thing. If there was a message to take or someone needed a hand, you didn’t question it much, you just went.

By late morning or after schoolwork when there was time, we’d be off down toward the river or into the bush with other kids. At the river it was simple things—swimming when it was warm, catching eels or fish when we were lucky, looking for watercress, and learning where it was safe and where it would turn on you. In the bush we’d roam tracks we half-made ourselves, gathering what we were allowed to gather, sometimes hunting small game, and always being told to leave things tidy—don’t break what you don’t need, don’t take more than your share. You learned to read the weather and the ground, and to keep quiet when you needed to.

Evenings were family time—back home before dark, wash up, help get the meal going, then sit and listen while the older ones talked. That’s where a lot of the real learning happened, hearing the stories and the reminders about respect, safety, and looking after each other. Then it was bed, because the next day was the same rhythm again.

I can really hear that steady rhythm in what you describe—work early, freedom outdoors when you could, and then the real lessons in the evenings.  

When you say “evenings were family time…sit and listen while the older ones talked,” what age were you around then, and who were those “older ones” in your household—parents, grandparents, aunties/uncles, or someone else?

I’d have been about seven or eight up through my early teens, so mostly late 1930s into the early 1940s. The “older ones” were mainly my parents, and there were older relatives around a lot too—an uncle or an aunty, and sometimes grandparents or other kaumātua would be in the house or would come by in the evenings. It wasn’t always a neat little nuclear household the way people talk about now; family drifted in and out, especially if there was work on or someone needed support.  

But whoever was there, the pattern was the same: kids kept quiet, listened, and you learned how to behave and how to look after people from the way they spoke and the stories they told.

Thank you—that’s very clear, and it really shows how learning happened through listening and watching.  

Of the elders you remember—an uncle, an aunty, grandparents or kaumātua—who stands out most to you, and what’s one story or teaching from them that has stayed with you all these years?

The one who stands out most to me is an older uncle of mine. He wasn’t a big talker, but when he did speak, everyone listened, because he’d been through hard times and he knew the bush and the river like they were part of him.

The teaching I’ve carried all these years is something he’d say when we were heading out: take only what you need, and leave the place so the next person can live from it too. He’d point out little things—a broken branch, a trampled bank, a half-done job—and he’d say, “That’s how trouble starts, not all at once, but bit by bit.” It wasn’t just about nature either; it was about people, the same idea—don’t take more than your share, and don’t leave a mess for someone else to clean up.

I think that’s why I ended up the way I did in forestry, always pushing safety and respect for the work, and later on trying to keep the language and the gatherings going. It’s that same responsibility, just in different clothing.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in a small community where everyone knew one another, and life was very centered around family and neighbors. As a child, things were simple but not always easy—there were times when resources were limited, so you learned to be practical and to share. I spent a lot of time helping at home and looking after younger children, which was very normal for girls then. Even so, there was warmth in it—women would check on each other, bring food if someone was sick, and talk things through together. Looking back, I think that sense of responsibility and community care stayed with me and shaped why I later felt drawn to maternal health work.
What was the name of the place you grew up—your town or village, and the country or region—and about what years were you living there as a child?

I grew up in a small village in northern Nigeria, in the Kano area. I lived there through my childhood, roughly from the early 1950s into the mid-to-late 1960s, before I left for training. I don’t always remember the exact years neatly, but that’s the period I think of when I picture my childhood home.
When you left your village for training, about how old were you, and where did you go for that training?

I was probably around 18 or 19 when I left home for training—old enough to feel excited, but still young enough to be a bit nervous about being away from everyone. I went into midwifery training, and I had to go into Kano city for it, because that’s where the larger training schools and hospitals were. I don’t always remember the exact name of the institution now, but it was in the Kano area, and it was the first time I’d lived somewhere that busy. It felt like a big step, but I knew it was the right one.
What do you remember most about that first move into Kano city for training—where you stayed (with relatives, a dormitory, a rented room), and what the biggest challenge was in those first weeks?

I stayed in a women’s hostel connected to the training—like a dormitory—with other young women who were also there to study. It wasn’t fancy, but it was safe, and in those days that mattered a lot to our families, especially for girls coming from the village.

What I remember most is the noise and the pace of Kano city. In my village you could recognize every face and you knew where you were going; in the city everything felt like it was moving at once. The biggest challenge in those first weeks was just adjusting—finding my way around, learning the routines at the hospital, and keeping up with the studying while I was also homesick. But after a little time, the other trainees became like sisters, and that helped me settle.
Where were you when you formed those close “like sisters” friendships—was it mainly in the hostel, in your classes, or during hospital shifts?

It started mainly in the hostel, because that’s where we were together at the end of the day—sharing meals, washing our clothes, talking quietly before sleeping. That’s where you really see who is kind, who will help you when you’re tired or missing home.

But it became strongest through a mix of the hostel and the hospital shifts. When you go through those long days in the wards together, learning and making mistakes and supporting each other, the bond becomes real. In class we were friendly, but it was more formal—hostel and shifts is where they became like sisters to me.
Do you remember any of those “like sisters” by name, and what kind of moments—either in the hostel or on a ward shift—made you feel, “yes, these are my people”?

I can still see their faces clearly, but to be honest the names don’t come as quickly now. There was one we used to call Hauwa, and another Aisha, but even with that I don’t want to pretend I remember every name perfectly after all these years.

The moments that made me feel “these are my people” were usually small things. In the hostel, if someone noticed you were quiet and homesick and they would share their food with you, or sit with you while you washed your clothes, just talking until your heart settled. And on the ward, when you were new and your hands were shaking a little, and someone would step in calmly—show you what to do without shaming you, and later explain it properly so you could do it yourself next time.

There were nights we were tired from shifts and still had studying to do, and one of them would say, “Come, we’ll read together,” and nobody was left behind. That kind of steady support—no gossip, no showing off—made me feel safe with them, like sisters, not just classmates.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]

transcript.content: Hello, thank you for agreeing to speak with me today. To get started, could you tell me a little bit about where you grew up and what your life was like as a child?

I grew up in France, in a household where books were treated almost like members of the family—always present, always respected. I wasn’t a particularly loud child; I was the sort who could disappear into a corner with a novel and be perfectly content for hours. School mattered a great deal in my home, and I remember teachers leaving a strong impression on me, especially the ones who made literature feel alive rather than just something to memorize. Even early on, I was drawn to plays and poetry—the rhythm of language, the way a scene could change how you saw people. Looking back, my childhood felt fairly simple, but it gave me that lifelong habit of observing and listening, which I think is what later made teaching feel so natural to me.
Thank you—that picture of a quiet, book-filled home really comes through. In which part of France did you grow up (town or region), and what was your home and neighborhood like there?

I grew up in the west of France, not too far from the Atlantic—more a small town than a big city. I tend to describe it as quiet and a bit inward-looking, the kind of place where people recognize you at the bakery and you learn the rhythm of the days by habit. Our home was modest but warm, with books everywhere—shelves in the main room, piles by the bed, and that sense that reading was simply part of being a decent person.

The neighborhood itself was mostly families and older people, not much noise, children playing in the street after school, and a certain calm that suited me. It wasn’t a place with grand cultural life on the surface, but it made me attentive—when life is simple, you notice details, and you listen more carefully to people. That atmosphere, I think, is what made literature feel like a secret door rather than an obligation.

I appreciate how clearly you describe that calm, inward-looking rhythm—and how books became a kind of “secret door” for you. About what years were you growing up there, and what was the name of the town (or the nearest larger town) near the Atlantic?

I would have been growing up there mostly in the late 1940s and through the 1950s—by the time the 1960s arrived, I was already moving on into studies and the beginning of adult life. As for the town, I’m a little hesitant to pin it to one name because it was small and, in my mind, it’s more a landscape and a feeling than a dot on a map. If I give a reference people recognize, it was in the orbit of La Rochelle, on that Atlantic side, without being in the city itself. That’s the closest “larger town” I usually mention when someone asks.

That makes sense—thank you for giving La Rochelle as a reference point. When the 1960s arrived and you “moved on into studies and the beginning of adult life,” where did you go to study, and what were you studying at first?

I went off to study in a larger city than the one I’d grown up in—still in western France at first, not too terribly far from home, though it felt like a different world to me then. I began with literature, very straightforwardly, and I was drawn especially to French classics and theatre. At that age I didn’t yet have a perfectly clear “plan,” but I knew I wanted to stay close to texts and to language. It was only as I went on that the idea of teaching stopped being a vague possibility and became something I could really see myself doing. I’m afraid I don’t always remember the exact names and dates as neatly as I once did, but that first step was definitely into literature.

I understand—and you’ve given a very clear sense of the direction even if the labels are fuzzy. In what city did you begin your studies, and at which institution (if you can recall), when teaching first started to feel like a real, concrete path for you?

I’m fairly sure I began in Poitiers, at the university there—what we would have simply called the Faculté des lettres at the time. I can’t swear to every formal name as it’s changed over the decades, but Poitiers is the city that stays in my mind as that first “larger” place where I studied literature seriously.

And teaching started to feel concrete for me a little later, still during those years—once I’d spent time around schools again, through placements and helping younger students, and I realized I didn’t just love the texts, I loved the moment when someone else suddenly understood them. I couldn’t give you an exact year without risking being wrong, but it was early in my university period, when the idea shifted from “maybe” to “yes, this is a life I can actually do.”

Thank you—that’s very vivid, especially the moment of seeing someone else suddenly understand a text. Where did those school placements and the helping of younger students take place—were they in Poitiers itself, or back nearer your home—and what kind of setting was it (a lycée, collège, or something else)?

Mostly it was in Poitiers itself, or just on the outskirts—close enough that I could still feel like a student with one foot in the university world and one foot in the classroom. I do remember it being a collège setting for at least part of it, because the ages and the energy were very particular: bright, restless, and suddenly receptive when you found the right way in.

The “helping younger students” was less formal than it sounds—more like tutoring and support for students who were struggling, sometimes arranged through contacts at the school, sometimes simply because a teacher knew I was studying literature and asked if I could give a hand. I may have gone back nearer my home once or twice during holidays and helped a little there too, but the experiences that really tipped me toward teaching happened around Poitiers, in those everyday classrooms where you could see the work of understanding happen in real time.
[INTERVIEW COMPLETE]
